Intesa Sanpaolo Sees Surge in US Market Operations
data.description:
(Alliance News) - Intesa Sanpaolo, through its IMI Corporate & Investment Banking division led by Mauro Micillo, is reinforcing its role as a financial bridge with the United States, as reported by Milano Finanza on Friday.

In 2025, IMI CIB supported international issuers in Yankee bond transactions totaling over USD 23 billion, and assisted American companies in euro-denominated issuances - known as Reverse Yankee - for approximately USD 10 billion.

According to MF, these transactions amount to more than USD 30 billion overall, underscoring the strength of the division's cross-market model.

By Claudia Cavaliere, Alliance News reporter

Comments and questions: redazione@alliancenews.com

BHP rolls out Australia's first battery-electric locomotives
data.description:
BHP Group Ltd - Melbourne, Australia-based diversified miner - Rolls out Australia's first brand new purpose-built battery-electric locomotives in the Port Hedland. The two BELs, developed with Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania-based Westinghouse Air Brake Technologies Corp, will be trialled on one of BHP's iron ore rail routes between its Pilbara mines and Port Hedland operations. Each locomotive houses a seven megawatt hour battery and uses regenerative braking to recapture energy on downhill runs. BHP says it supports the company's efforts to cut Scope 1 and 2 emissions across its iron ore rail network. Commissioning is underway ahead of track trials to test performance in live operations.

Tim Day, BHP Western Australia Iron Ore Asset president, says: "It marks the beginning of an important trial to understand how this technology can help us reduce diesel use, lower associated operational greenhouse gas emissions and improve efficiency across our rail network."

Current stock price: AUD42.75

12-month change: up 6.9%

By Judy Amaca, Alliance News reporter Asia-Pacific

Comments and questions to newsroom@alliancenews.com

Copyright 2025 Alliance News Ltd. All Rights Reserved.

Green Criticism and Aviation Praise for Black-Red Coalition Decisions
data.description:
BERLIN (dpa-AFX) - Criticism from the Greens and environmentalists, and praise from the aviation sector: The results of the coalition committee have sparked mixed reactions to the black-red government alliance. The announcement to reverse the air travel ticket tax increase, previously decided by the former traffic light coalition, has been particularly divisive. The leaders of the CDU, CSU, and SPD held discussions lasting around three and a half hours in the evening. Some of their plans had already been fundamentally announced in advance.

Who is voicing criticism

Green Party leader Felix Banaszak accused Chancellor Friedrich Merz (CDU) and the coalition leaders from CDU, CSU, and SPD of lacking courage. "Believing that the economic crisis can be solved by slashing the air travel tax and creating a 'Germany Fund' is quite presumptuous," he said.

Greenpeace called the reversal of the air travel tax increase a disastrous signal, referencing the ongoing world climate conference in Brazil: "While the international community at COP30 in Belém is struggling to reduce CO2 emissions, the German government is handing out tax and price breaks to fossil industries," German Greenpeace chief Martin Kaiser told Deutsche Presse-Agentur. He said this would further fuel global warming and undermine Germany's credibility.

Who is voicing praise

The aviation industry, on the other hand, predictably welcomed the planned reduction in ticket taxes. Their associations praised it as an important step to strengthen the sector and Germany as an aviation hub.

Ralph Beisel, CEO of the ADV airport association, called it "the beginning of a true trend reversal." His counterpart at the industry association BDL, Joachim Lang, added, "For Germany to benefit from the ongoing aviation boom in Europe, further steps will be needed in the coming years." Lufthansa stated that the government has shown it understands the seriousness of the situation in the aviation sector.

What the ticket tax is about

The coalition plans to lower the tax as of July 1, 2026. Merz mentioned a figure of about 350 million euros in favor of Germany's aviation industry. Additionally, measures will be taken to ensure that air traffic control fees do not rise further in 2026, but are instead reduced for the first time. The tax on departures from German airports was introduced in 2011 by the then black-yellow government to help balance the budget.

Currently, the tax per flight is 15.53 euros for domestic and short-haul flights, 39.34 euros for medium-haul, and 70.83 euros for long-haul destinations. The airlines are responsible for paying these amounts.

The industry has long called for a reduction in the air travel tax. International airlines are increasingly basing their aircraft at locations with lower entry costs, to the detriment of German airports. Domestic flights and direct routes from smaller and medium-sized airports to destinations abroad have been particularly affected in Germany.

What else was decided: Industrial electricity price

As of January 1, 2026, a state-subsidized, lower industrial electricity price will be introduced until 2028. Merz had already announced this at the "Steel Summit" a week ago. He mentioned a target price of 5 cents per kilowatt hour. This will apply to energy-intensive companies facing international competition, such as the steel and chemical industries.

Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil, who participated in the meeting as SPD chairman and vice chancellor, estimated costs at three to five billion euros, to be financed from the Climate and Transformation Fund, a special federal fund. Banaszak criticized this financing approach: "An industrial electricity price is the right move. But it has nothing to do with climate protection."

New gas-fired power plants

These plants are intended to serve as reserves in the future, stepping in when renewable energy sources cannot meet demand due to lack of sun or wind. To make investments worthwhile for energy companies, state support will be provided, pending approval from the EU Commission. The coalition expects the green light soon.

Merz said that next year, eight gigawatts should be put out to tender, with the plants to be operational by 2031. They should be technically capable of using hydrogen and, in line with climate goals, be fully decarbonized by 2045 at the latest, meaning they operate without carbon emissions.

Germany Fund

Under its umbrella, several financing instruments outlined in the coalition agreement will be linked to stimulate private investment. Klingbeil said a "docking point" should be created for private capital, for example in energy, resilience, or start-ups in security policy. Germany lags internationally in financing young, innovative companies./and/DP/mis

data.title: South Korea's president Lee to attend G20 in Johannesburg, his office says
EMEA Morning Briefing : Bets for Fed December Rate Cut Recede
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Watch For:

EU GDP, trade; trading updates from Allianz, Richemont, Swiss Re, Land Securities Group

Opening Call:

European stock futures were broadly lower in tandem with the declines in Asian stock benchmarks. U.S. Treasurys and the dollar were little changed. Gold and oil rose.

Equities:

European equity futures were tracking broadly lower early Friday. The end of the longest U.S. government shutdown in history should have drawn a sigh of relief from investors. But the respite from the reopening was overshadowed by rising concerns over AI stock valuations and receding expectations for a December rate cut by the Fed, leading to U.S. stocks' worst day in a month on Thursday.

"Investors are taking some profits in megacap tech after an extended AI driven run and reallocating toward more reasonably valued sectors," said David Miller, chief investment officer and senior portfolio manager at Catalyst Funds.

Investors, economists, and policymakers have been operating without crucial U.S. government data for more than 40 days. "We anticipate ongoing challenges with obtaining a clean read -- we think Q4 data will likely add more confusion than clarity," said Mike Reid, senior U.S. economist at RBC.

Meanwhile, data released earlier today showed that China's economic growth momentum slowed in October, weighed down by last year's high base when Beijing rolled out stimulus measures to support a cooling economy.

Forex:

The euro could rise above the key $1.20 level next year from $1.1637 currently, said ING analyst Francesco Pesole. The eurozone should see a meaningful pick up in economic growth as fiscal stimulus takes hold, he said.

A potential fall in oil prices could also benefit the euro. "The euro-dollar exchange rate's fair value in the medium term is heavily reliant on commodity prices though its terms of trade."

ING also expects the European Central Bank is finished cutting interest rates while the Federal Reserve could cut rates by a further 75 basis points. It sees the euro rising to $1.22 by the fourth quarter of 2026.

Bonds:

Treasury yields were little changed amid increasing bets on a Fed rate hold next month as the end of the government shutdown leaves doubts about the publication of relevant economic indicators.

Trump's economic adviser Kevin Hassett said October unemployment rate may never be released because the monthly survey it is extracted from wasn't conducted. The data flow is expected to resume in coming days.

The CME's FedWatch tool puts odds of a December cut as roughly 50-50, and no longer the slam-dunk it seemed to be a few weeks ago.

Energy:

Oil gained amid possible position adjustments. The oil market continues to digest the OPEC's latest outlook, Sucden Financial's Research team said. This outlook now indicates global supply and demand will be roughly balanced by 2026.

"We expect this shift, combined with persistent strength in non-OPEC production, to keep crude range-bound in the near term." Oil prices' direction will probably be increasingly tied to macroeconomic sentiment rather than supply shocks, the team added.

Metals:

Gold edged higher on possible dip-buying. "People are still expecting to see continued central bank buying," said Fawad Razaqzada, market analyst for Global Macro at FOREX.com. However, "I would actually be on the lookout for a bit of profit-taking to take prices back down to near $4,000/oz in coming days and weeks," Razaqzada said.

--

Base metals were lower. Speculative positioning appears to be driving cross-commodity sentiment, Sucden Financial said. "We expect that previous highs will now act as robust resistances, as markets struggle to find new fundamental catalysts strong enough to sustain a lasting breakout," they said.

The U.S. dollar regaining some conviction could also cap upside appetite for base metals.

--

Iron ore gained. "Prices are expected to fluctuate in the short term, with no clear driver in sight," Nanhua Futures said. Meanwhile, markets may continue to weigh supply concerns. "The market has been fretting about the imminent startup of the Simandou iron ore mines in Guinea," ANZ Research analysts said, adding that "at 10% of global demand at its peak, the mine could swamp the market."

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

China's Economic Growth Momentum Slowed in October

China's economic growth momentum slowed in October, weighed down by a high base from the previous year when Beijing rolled out stimulus measures to support a cooling economy, according to official data released on Friday.

Industrial production rose 4.9% in October compared to a year earlier, a decline from the 6.5% increase in September, the National Bureau of Statistics said.

U.S. to Cut Tariffs on Bananas, Coffee and Other Goods From Four Countries

The U.S. plans to eliminate tariffs on bananas, coffee, beef and certain apparel and textile products under framework agreements with four Latin American nations, a senior administration official told reporters Thursday.

The expected move-which would apply to some goods from Ecuador, Argentina, El Salvador and Guatemala-is part of a shift from the Trump administration to water down some of its so-called reciprocal tariffs in the face of rising prices for consumers, as well as legal uncertainty after a Supreme Court hearing this month.

South Korea to Expand Support for Tariff-Hit Auto Industry

South Korea plans to expand policy support for its auto industry in the wake of a trade deal struck between Seoul and Washington.

The export-dependent economy is planning more than 15 trillion South Korean won, equivalent to $10.2 billion, of support measures for domestic manufacturers of cars and car parts. These include new loan programs and tariff quotas for raw materials used in manufacturing, the prime minister's office said Friday.

Germany's Answer to Its Conscription Dilemma: a Database of Young Men Fit for War

BERLIN-Germany will build a database of young people detailing their fitness, aptitude and outlook to help it pick whom to draft should the country be attacked.

The proposed move, a step toward reintroducing military conscription, comes as countries across Europe grapple with how to repopulate their armed forces under pressure from Washington and an expansionist Russia that European capitals accuse of waging a hybrid war on the continent.

EU Investigates Alphabet's Google over Publishers' Ranking Policy

The European Commission opened a new investigation into Alphabet's Google over how the tech giant ranks news publishers in search results.

The commission said Thursday that it is concerned that Google might be demoting news media and other publishers' websites and content in search results when they include content from third-party commercial partners. The European Union's executive arm said Google's so-called site reputation abuse policy could impede publishers from legitimately making money online through third-party content.

Amazon and Microsoft Back Effort That Would Restrict Nvidia's Exports to China

WASHINGTON-Amazon.com is joining Microsoft in supporting legislation that threatens to further limit Nvidia's ability to export to China, a rare split between the chip designer and two of its biggest customers.

The moves by Microsoft and Amazon to work against a company they are deeply intertwined with highlights the fierce nature of the artificial-intelligence race. The companies are all jockeying for favorable policy to stay ahead of competitors. Nvidia is fighting for access to the lucrative Chinese market despite security concerns.

Verizon to Cut About 15,000 Jobs

Verizon Communications is planning to cut roughly 15,000 jobs, looking to reduce costs as it contends with increased competition for both wireless service and home internet customers, according to people familiar with the matter.

The cuts, the largest ever for the carrier, are set to take place in the next week, the people said. The majority of the reduction is expected to be made through layoffs.

Write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

Shares of JD Dropped as Profit Dragged by New Businesses
data.description:
By Tracy Qu


JD.com shares weakened in Hong Kong after the Chinese e-commerce giant's profits were hit by heavy spending on its food delivery and other new businesses.

Its Hong Kong-listed shares were down 5.6% at midday on Friday, on track for their biggest daily decline since April. In comparison, the Hang Seng Tech Index declined 2.2%.

The company late Thursday said that its net profit in three months ended September dropped 55% compared with the same period a year earlier. Third quarter revenue rose 15% on year.

JD.com, which still considers e-commerce its primary business, has moved quickly to gain a foothold in the food-delivery sector. It entered the fast-growing market earlier this year, using heavy subsidies to lure customers away from Meituan, the market leader, and Ele.me, the No. 2 player.

The company's profits are likely to remain pressured, according to HSBC analysts in a research note. JD is likely to "step up subsidies to defend sales," they say.

Intense investment in its budget shopping platform Jingxi and overseas business is likely to be sustained in the short run, they say. HSBC retains a buy rating on its ADRs but trims their target price to $39.00 from $40.00. JD.com's ADRs last traded at $30.71.


Write to Tracy Qu at tracy.qu@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-14-25 0006ET
